stem: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, if \( F \) is an antiderivative of \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \), then \( \displaystyle\int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} f(x)\,dx = F([[hi]]) - F([[lo]]) \). Compute this definite integral.
solution: An antiderivative is \( F(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{3}x^{3} + \dfrac{[[b]]}{2}x^{2} + [[c]]x \). By FTC, \( \displaystyle\int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} f(x)\,dx = F([[hi]]) - F([[lo]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f \) itself at the upper limit instead of the antiderivative \( F \).
This subtracts values of \( f \) (the integrand) at the two limits instead of values of its antiderivative \( F \).
This wrongly applies a cubic power-rule shortcut to the whole integrand as if it were a single power of \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Let \( g(x) = \displaystyle\int_{[[lo]]}^{x} \left([[a]]t^{2}+[[b]]t+[[c]]\right)dt \). By Part 1 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, find \( g'([[x0]]) \).
solution: By FTC Part 1, \( g'(x) = [[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]x+[[c]] \) (the integrand itself, evaluated at \( x \), with the lower limit \( [[lo]] \) playing no role in the derivative). So \( g'([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]])^{2}+[[b]]([[x0]])+[[c]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly subtracts a contribution from the fixed lower limit, which does not affect the derivative of an accumulation function.
This differentiates the integrand ITSELF again, one derivative too many.
This evaluates the integrand at the fixed lower limit \( [[lo]] \) instead of at \( [[x0]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Let \( g(x) = \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{x^{2}} \left([[c]]t+[[d]]\right)dt \). Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus together with the chain rule, find \( g'([[x0]]) \).
solution: Let \( F \) be an antiderivative of \( [[c]]t+[[d]] \), so \( g(x)=F(x^{2})-F([[a]]) \). By FTC and the chain rule, \( g'(x) = f(x^{2})\cdot 2x = \left([[c]]x^{2}+[[d]]\right)\cdot 2x \). Evaluating this derivative at \( x=[[x0]] \) (equivalently, differentiating \( F(x^{2})=\tfrac{[[c]]}{2}x^{4}+[[d]]x^{2} \) directly) gives \( g'([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies FTC Part 1 as if the upper limit were \( x \) itself, forgetting the chain-rule factor from the \( x^{2} \) limit.
This uses the correct chain-rule structure but with \( f \) evaluated at \( x \) instead of at \( x^{2} \).
This multiplies the integrand's linear term by \( 2x \) but drops the constant term \( [[d]] \) before doing so.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: Let \( g(x) = \displaystyle\int_{x}^{x^{2}} [[c]]t\,dt \). Using the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus together with the chain rule on BOTH (variable) limits, find \( g'([[x0]]) \).
solution: Let \( F \) be an antiderivative of \( [[c]]t \), so \( g(x) = F(x^{2}) - F(x) \). By FTC and the chain rule on each limit, \( g'(x) = [[c]]x^{2}\cdot 2x - [[c]]x\cdot 1 \) (upper-limit term minus lower-limit term, each with its own chain-rule factor). Equivalently, differentiating \( F(x^{2})-F(x) = \tfrac{[[c]]}{2}x^{4} - \tfrac{[[c]]}{2}x^{2} \) directly gives \( g'([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gets the upper-limit chain-rule term right but forgets the chain-rule factor of \( 1 \) is still a subtraction, not addition, for the lower limit.
This applies FTC to each limit but forgets the chain-rule multiplier \( 2x \) on the upper-limit term entirely.
This adds the two limit contributions instead of subtracting the lower-limit term.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.

stem: A particle moves along a line with velocity \( v(t) = [[a]]t^{2} + [[b]] \) (in meters per second). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the particle's net displacement from \( t=[[t1]] \) to \( t=[[t2]] \) seconds is \( \displaystyle\int_{[[t1]]}^{[[t2]]} v(t)\,dt \). Find this net displacement (in meters).
solution: An antiderivative of \( v(t) \) is \( s(t)=\tfrac{[[a]]}{3}t^{3}+[[b]]t \). By FTC, the net displacement is \( s([[t2]])-s([[t1]]) = [[answer]] \) meters.
distractor_rationales: This reports the instantaneous velocity at \( t=[[t2]] \), not the accumulated displacement over the interval.
This subtracts velocities at the two endpoints instead of integrating the velocity function over the interval.
This wrongly plugs the time interval's length directly into \( v(t) \) rather than integrating.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
